coredata.dc:title: UHPLC combined with mass spectrometric study of as-synthesized carbon dots samples
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiaojuan
Chin Paau, Man
Hu, Qin
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Choi, Martin M.F.
coredata.dc:description: A fast and green approach to synthesize carbon dots (C-dots) by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of precursor chitosan as the carbon source and glacial acetic acid as the condensation agent has been developed. Ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) has been applied to study C-dots samples prepared with various synthetic conditions including amount of chitosan, concentration of acetic acid and reaction time. All the as-prepared C-dots samples are complex mixtures of C-dots species which can be separated by UHPLC within 16min. All the separated C-dots peaks display a distinctive absorption bands at 261–271nm corresponding to the n→π* transition of C=O bond. The obtained chromatograms indicate the composition and complexity of an as-synthesized C-dots sample which is commonly neglected. In addition, high-performance liquid chromatography is employed to fractionate the C-dots sample. The separated C-dots fractions are collected and characterized by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS). The PL spectra of the fractions display emission peaks at 427–446nm upon excitation at 340nm. The C-dots fractions are fully anatomized by MALDI-TOF MS, displaying their fragmentation mass ion features and indicating the surface functionalities of C-dots. The findings highlight the virtues of UHPLC to separate and reveal the unique characteristics of individual C-dots product which may have potential applications in the fields of bioanalysis, bioimaging, catalysis, chemosensing, energy storage, and optoelectronics device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UHPLC
Mass spectrometry
Carbon dots sample
HPLC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of C-dots sample and HPLC fractions
Effect of chitosan, glacial acetic acid and reaction time on C-dots product
Effect of MeOH content on separation of C-dots by UHPLC
PL analysis of HPLC C-dots fractions
MALDI-TOF MS analysis of HPLC C-dots fractions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution histogram of C-dots. The inset in (A) displays the selected area electron diffraction pattern.
(A) XPS survey scan, (B) C1s, (C) O1s and (D) N1s XPS spectra of C-dots.
UHPLC analysis of C-dots prepared with various synthesis conditions: (A) amount of chitosan, (B) concentration of acetic acid, and (C) reaction time. The chromatograms are monitored at 275nm.

PL spectra of fractions 1–15 at different λ
ex (300–400nm).
MALDI-TOF MS of fractions 1–15. The insets are the expanded MS. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: UHPLC combined with mass spectrometric study of as-synthesized carbon dots samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Exchange student on visit to Hong Kong Baptist University.
Present address: Acadia Divinity College, Acadia University, 15 University Avenue, Wolfville, Nova Scotia, Canada B4P 2R6.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and green approach to synthesize carbon dots (C-dots) by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of precursor chitosan as the carbon source and glacial acetic acid as the condensation agent has been developed. Ultra-high performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) has been applied to study C-dots samples prepared with various synthetic conditions including amount of chitosan, concentration of acetic acid and reaction time. All the as-prepared C-dots samples are complex mixtures of C-dots species which can be separated by UHPLC within 16min. All the separated C-dots peaks display a distinctive absorption bands at 261–271nm corresponding to the n→π* transition of C=O bond. The obtained chromatograms indicate the composition and complexity of an as-synthesized C-dots sample which is commonly neglected. In addition, high-performance liquid chromatography is employed to fractionate the C-dots sample. The separated C-dots fractions are collected and characterized by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS). The PL spectra of the fractions display emission peaks at 427–446nm upon excitation at 340nm. The C-dots fractions are fully anatomized by MALDI-TOF MS, displaying their fragmentation mass ion features and indicating the surface functionalities of C-dots. The findings highlight the virtues of UHPLC to separate and reveal the unique characteristics of individual C-dots product which may have potential applications in the fields of bioanalysis, bioimaging, catalysis, chemosensing, energy storage, and optoelectronics device.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanostructures such as carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, nanofibers, nanodiamonds, graphene and other carbonaceous nanomaterials are developing nanoparticles (NP) free of cadmium, lead, and mercury, which are attracting considerable interest for their many unique and novel properties in the electronic, optical, catalytic and biological applications [1–3]. Among these carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (C-dots) can be considered as one of the rising stars for research and development. C-dots are composed of quasispherical, nanocrystalline, or amorphous carbon structures less than 10nm in size [4]. The major attributes of C-dots are their simplicity of synthesis, low-cost, nontoxicity, non-blinking, low photobleaching, high photoactivity, and large two-photon cross-section properties [5–7]. While C-dots are being explored for applications exploiting their intrinsic luminescence [4,8], the cause of the luminescence is not fully understood. So far the explanation of excitation (λex )-dependent [9,10] or -independent [11–13] photoluminescence (PL) of C-dots and the origin of PL in C-dots can be generally classified into two main categories: core [14–16] and surface-state related emissions [4,6]. Krysmann et al. [17] has discussed the dual emission of C-dots, owing to both carbogenic core and surface molecular fluorophores.

The outstanding properties and potential applications of C-dots have led to a wealth of methods for obtaining C-dots [18–21]. To date, many efforts have been made on the fabrication of C-dots such as chemical and thermal oxidation, laser ablation, electrochemical etching, microwave method, and ultrasonic synthesis [6,7,14,22–25]. But most synthesis methods are deficient in accurate control of lateral dimensions and surface chemistry of C-dots [26]. A facile and high-output strategy for fabrication of C-dots has been proposed which involves applying microwave energy to carbonize chitosan and acetic acid [20,27]. Herein, we attempt to employ ultra-high liquid chromatography (UHPLC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) to separate, fractionate and characterize as-synthesized C-dots samples prepared with chitosan and acetic acid under various synthesis conditions of microwave time, quantities of acetic acid and chitosan.

Owing to the intense interest in the synthesis and applications of the above-mentioned C-dots, there is a need for analytical methodology that can provide efficient separation and fractionation of these materials. Previously, graphene oxide and its reduction to chemically converted graphene can be monitored by capillary electrophoresis (CE) [28]. C-dots samples derived from soot have also been separated and fractioned by gel electrophoresis [14] and CE [29]. However, a limited amount of work addressing relatively high-resolution analysis coupled with the practical ability to collect significant amounts of C-dots is being done.

Recently Vinci et al. analyzed C-dots by anion-exchange (AE)-HPLC [30,31]. Unfortunately, AE-HPLC column only applies to separate charged components but is unable to handle neutral C-dots. Reversed-phase (RP)-HPLC is a better analytical separation technique in dealing with a complex mixture of neutral and charged C-dots. Compared with AE-HPLC, RP-HPLC column possesses higher separation efficiency and is less expensive. Thus, it is more popular and therefore our research group has devoted efforts to separate C-dots by RP-HPLC [32–35]. However, UHPLC is superior to HPLC in terms of speed of separation, separation efficiency, sample loading, and solvent consumption. As such, UHPLC will be an ideal analytical separation method for studying C-dots samples prepared from different synthetic conditions.

For UHPLC, the collected fractions of C-dots would not be sufficient for further characterization because the sample loading is small. Higher concentration and larger size of sample loading resulted in peak broadening and tailing, leading to poor separation of C-dots fractions. Thus, we chose HPLC as a tool for collecting C-dots fractions for photoluminescence (PL) and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) studies. MS is the most powerful tool to analyze the structure and surface morphology of C-dots samples. The major contribution of this work is to develop and apply UHPLC methodology to study the effect of synthetic conditions on the quality of as-prepared C-dots samples. Our proposed UHPLC methodology provides a fast route for better understanding of an as-prepared C-dots sample.

Chitosan (85%, 100,000–300,000Da) was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Glacial acetic acid was obtained from Fisher Chemicals (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). HPLC-grade methanol (MeOH) was purchased from Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). 2,5-Dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB, 98%) was from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Water was purified through a Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm. All reagents of analytical reagent grade or above were used as received without further purification.

C-dots samples were synthesized according to the literature methods [28,29] with slight modifications. In brief, chitosan was mixed with acetic acid under vigorous stirring in a glass vial. The solution was then heated in a domestic microwave oven (800W), during which the formation of C-dots was indicated by the change from a colorless colloidal solution to brown solid. After the reaction, the glass vial was cooled to room temperature. The brown solid was dissolved in 10mL distilled de-ionized (DDI) water and sonicated in a water bath. The brown solution was filtered to remove any unreacted solid or side-products and the brown filtrate was dialyzed through a Spectrum Laboratories dialysis membrane tube with MWCO of 500–1000Da (Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) in a 1L DDI water with stirring and recharging with fresh DDI water every 24h over a course of 3 days. Finally, the clear and yellowish aqueous solution containing C-dots was dried by a stream of nitrogen (N2) at <50°C to obtain the deep yellow C-dots sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of C-dots sample and HPLC fractions
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of the HPLC C-dots fractions were acquired on a QM4 Photon Technology International spectrofluorometer (Birmingham, NJ, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of C-dots sample was conducted on a Nicolet Magna 550 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) with the scan range 400–4000cm−1. The C-dots/potassium bromide (KBr) and chitosan/KBr disks were obtained by mixing few milligrams of C-dots and chitosan with ~100mg KBr powder, respectively, grinding to approximately 2μm in an agate mortar, and subjecting to 29.7MPa pressure by a pressing machine.

The TEM image of the C-dots sample was acquired on a JEOL JEM-1011 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples were prepared by casting and evaporating a droplet of aqueous solution of HPLC fraction onto Agar Scientific 300 mesh holey carbon-coated copper grids (Stansted, Essex, UK). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern was also acquired in TEM. Particle size was determined by Image J software.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the C-dots sample were acquired on an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan) with AlKα radiation operating at 1486.6eV. Spectra were processed by the Case XPS v.2.3.12 software using a peak-fitting routine with symmetrical Gaussian–Lorentzian functions.

The concentrated HPLC C-dots fractions were analyzed by a Bruker Autoflex MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). A small aliquot of HPLC fraction was mixed with a 1.0M solution of DHB in MeOH/H2O (1:1 v/v). Then 4.0µL of this mixture was deposited on a MALDI target plate and air-dried. The sample was inserted into the instrument and irradiated by a pulsed N2 laser working at 337nm (3ns pulse, 3Hz). In general, 30 laser shots were averaged for each spectrum.

UHPLC separation of C-dots samples were conducted on a Waters Acquity UPLC H-class system in conjunction with a photodiode array (PDA) detector (Milford, MA, USA). The absorption spectra of the separated peaks were taken at 220–600nm. The chromatographic Acquity UPLC column (2.1mm i.d.×150mm) was packed with 1.7µm C18 bonded silica with 130Å pore size (BEH-C18, Waters Technologies). The C-dots solutions in mobile phases were prefiltered through 0.45µm cellulose acetate membrane syringe filters (Alltech Associates Inc., Deerfield, IL, USA) before injections. The mobile phase comprising MeOH and Milli-Q water was filtered through a 0.45µm cellulose acetate membrane filter (Alltech) prior to use. The injection volume was 1.0µL and the column temperature was maintained at 25°C. A gradient elution program was used at a flow rate of 0.30mL/min and was applied as follows: 0.0% v/v MeOH linearly increased to 13.0% v/v MeOH from 0.0 to 16min.

Chromatographic fractionation of C-dots sample was conducted on a Waters HPLC instrument consisting of a model 2695 separations module capable of gradient elution and a model 2996 PDA. Absorption spectra (1.2nm resolution) were taken at 220–450nm. An Agilent TC-C18 chromatographic column (4.6mm i.d.×250mm) packed with 5µm C18 bonded silica with 130Å pore size was used for HPLC separation (Santa Clara, CA, USA). The C-dots sample solution was prefiltered through an Alltech 0.45µm cellulose acetate membrane syringe filter before injection.

The mobile phase comprising MeOH and Milli-Q water was filtered through an Alltech 0.45µm cellulose acetate membrane filter prior to use. The injection volume was 20µL and the column temperature was maintained at 25°C. A gradient elution program was used at a flow rate of 0.80mL/min and was applied as 0.0% v/v MeOH linearly increased to 15% v/v MeOH from 0.0 to 29min. The chromatogram was monitored at 275nm and the separated C-dots HPLC fractions were collected manually based on appropriate signal threshold. The collected HPLC fractions were purged with a stream of N2 at room temperature to concentrated C-dots solutions prior to PL and MS studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chitosan, glacial acetic acid and reaction time on C-dots product
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the synthesis conditions can affect the quality of the as-prepared C-dots product. Tables S1 and S2 display the effect of various synthesis conditions such as quantity of chitosan, concentration of acetic acid and reaction time on the quantum yield (Φ
S ) of the as-prepared C-dots samples. The syntheses of C-dots are reproducible. The determination of Φ
S is described in Supplementary Data. The Φ
S of C-dots increases gradually with the increase in chitosan as shown in Table S1. When the amount of chitosan is over 1.0g, the Φ
S of C-dots gradually drops. Table S1 also displays the effect of glacial acetic acid on the Φ
S of C-dots. In 2.0% glacial acetic acid, the Φ
S of C-dots is the highest since chitosan dissolves better. The solubility of chitosan declines gradually along with the increase in acetic acid. Thus, the Φ
S of C-dots drops when the concentration of acetic acid goes over 2.0%. The effect of reaction time on the Φ
S of C-dots is shown in Table S2. 15-min microwave-heating is the best reaction time as it produces the highest Φ
S of C-dots. Too short or too long reaction time leads to either incomplete or excessive carbonization. In summary, the optimal synthesis conditions were found to be 1.00g chitosan and 15.0mL 2.0% acetic acid under 15-min microwave-heating which produces the highest Φ
s (5.33%) of C-dots sample.

Fig. S1A depicts the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the C-dots sample synthesized with 1.0g chitosan, 15mL 2.0% acetic acid and 15-min microwave-heating. C-dots exhibits one typical absorption peak at 275nm corresponding to the n→π* transition of C=O bond is observed [16,36]. Fig. S1B displays the PL spectra of C-dots under various λ
ex . The PL spectra were bathochromatically shifted from 431 to 482nm with the λ
ex switching from 280 to 400nm. This bathochromic shift can be explained by the presence of various C-dots species in the sample [20]. The representative TEM image of the as-synthesized C-dots sample is depicted in Fig. 1A. The C-dots are mostly of spherical morphology and disperse rather evenly on the TEM grid surface. The corresponding histogram obtained by statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using the ImageJ software is displayed in Fig. 1B, revealing a broad size distribution of C-dots. The corresponding particle size distribution of C-dots is 1.75–4.75nm with an average diameter of 3.13±0.4nm.

IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the surface functionality of C-dots. Fig. S2 depicts the IR spectra of C-dots sample (Fig. S2A) and chitosan (Fig. S2B). A broad absorption peak attributing to the O–H stretching (~3356–3499cm−1) and sharp absorption peaks corresponding to C–H (2931cm−1), C–O (1138cm−1), and C–O–C stretching (1028cm−1) are found for C-dots sample. Chitosan is believed to be the carbon source of these functional groups. The IR spectrum of chitosan possessing these functionalities is displayed in Fig. S2B. In addition, the IR spectrum of C-dots sample possesses distinctive absorption peak at 1630cm−1, indicating the formation of C=C unsaturated bonds in the carbon cores which are consistent with other fluorescent C-dots [37,38]. More importantly, the characteristic absorption peak amido CON–H bending (1568cm−1) and amido CO–N (1430cm−1) stretching are identified in C-dots, suggesting the presence of amido functionalities on C-dots. The IR spectrum clearly indicates that the amidation reaction between chitosan and acetic acid took place during the C-dots synthesis, resulting in the formation of C-dots with a structure comprising sp 2- and sp 3-hybridized carbon cores and amido and carboxylic acid groups on the surface. These observations are further confirmed by the MS analysis (vide infra).

XPS were acquired to gain further insight into the surface functional groups and elemental states of C-dots. Fig. 2A depicts the survey scan of C-dots. The C-dots show three apparent peaks centered at 285.0, 400.5, and 532.0eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Fig. 2B illustrates the C1s XPS spectrum of C-dots which is deconvoluted into five peaks at 284.75, 285.45, 286.3, 287.75, and 288.65eV corresponding to C=N, C–H, C=O, N–C=O, and O–C=O, respectively [39–41]. The O1s of C-dots spectrum (Fig. 2C) exhibits three peaks at 531.5, 532.5, and 532.96eV corresponding to O–C=O, C=O, and N–O groups, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) of C-dots shows three peaks at 399.2, 399.7 and 400.2eV attributing to the H–N–(C)2, N–H and C–N functionalities, respectively. These results of XPS concur with the IR spectroscopy (vide supra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MeOH content on separation of C-dots by UHPLC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3 depicts the effect of MeOH in the mobile phase on UHPLC separation of a C-dots sample (synthesized with 1.0g chitosan and 15mL 2.0% acetic acid and 15-min microwave-heating). When MeOH is 15% v/v, the separation of C-dots is unsatisfactory with only one single intense peak, comprising numerous co-eluted C-dots species. Fortunately, the resolutions of these overlapping peaks are improved by lowering the MeOH content in the mobile phase. The chromatograms show that lower organic solvent content tends to increase the retention of the C-dots species, suggesting that the UHPLC separation of the C-dots follows the typical RP separation behavior of solutes on a BEH-C18 column. In brief, the overall non-polarity of the mobile phase drops considerably with lower MeOH content, allowing for better hydrophobic interaction between the C-dots and C18 stationary phase. By contrast, increasing the MeOH content in the mobile phase will considerably shorten the retention time of C-dots. In essence, MeOH has a significant impact on the separation of the as-synthesized C-dots sample. Although full aqueous mobile phase seems to be ideal for the separation of C-dots sample, the retention times of the later eluting solutes are excessively long (>50min) and they tend to strongly retain on the column. As such, a solvent program with progressive increase of MeOH in the mobile phase has to be employed to completely resolve and elute all the C-dots species in an as-synthesized C-dots sample. Herein, a gradient elution program (0.0% v/v MeOH linearly increased to 13.0% v/v MeOH from 0.0 to 16min) has been successfully developed to separate most C-dots species in the sample (vide infra).

For all C-dots synthesized by different synthesis conditions, an absorption peak at ca. 275nm corresponding to the n→π* transition of C=O bond is observed [16,36]. The emission peaks (λ
em ) of these C-dots remain constant at ca. 412–421nm when λ
ex is at 340nm. This shows these C-dots possess similar UV–vis absorption and PL properties. In addition, UHPLC-PDA analysis was also applied to these C-dots samples as this can give a better idea of the compositions of C-dots samples rather than by monitoring the Φ
S of the as-synthesized C-dots samples alone. The chromatograms and their peak spectra can indicate the complexity of C-dots samples under different synthesis conditions. Fig. 3 depicts the absorption chromatograms of C-dots synthesized with different amounts of chitosan, concentrations of acetic acid and microwave-heating times. In the absorption chromatogram, every separation peak represents a type of C-dots species in an as-synthesized product. Although these C-dots samples synthesized by different synthesis conditions possess similar UV–vis absorption spectra and PL properties, they may contain different C-dots species. In theory, it is better to monitor the compositions of the C-dots products by UHPLC than that of Φ
S when various synthesis conditions are applied. In this work, the C-dots sample synthesized with 1.0g chitosan, 15mL 2.0% acetic acid and 15-min microwave-heating was chosen for most studies as it can be separated by UHPLC and the number of peaks is not too excessive.

Fig. 4A displays the representative UHPLC chromatogram of the C-dots sample synthesized with 1.0g chitosan and 15mL 2.0% acetic acid with 15-min microwave-heating. For comparison, a water sample was injected (curve a) and no peak is observed. By contrast, numerous well separated peaks are found in the absorption chromatogram (curve b) of the C-dots sample. No peaks are eluted after 16min for the C-dots sample. Under the optimal solvent program, more later-eluted peaks are observed as compared to the chromatogram obtained from isocratic elution (Fig. S3). At least 15 elution peaks are found in the chromatogram and are labeled as 1–15. Fig. 4B displays the UV–visible absorption spectra of the UHPLC fractions 1–15 (from bottom to top), revealing the spectral characteristics of these C-dots fractions. These spectra are normalized at 220nm to remove the effect of concentration difference and are offset to allow for focus on spectral shape and band position. The inset in Fig. 4B indicates the absorption peak of each fraction. The absorption spectra show typical spectral characteristics of the C-dots sample. All spectra display absorption peaks at 261–273nm corresponding to the n→π* transition of C=O bond. However, fractions 2–5, 7, 8, 11, and 14 were eluted at different times but have similar spectral features. A possible reason is that they have similar carbon cores but various surface functionalities. Similarly, fractions 1 and 6, and fractions 10, 12, 13, 15 possess similar carbon cores but different surface structures.

The MS analyses show similar results with that of absorption spectra (vide infra). Both absorption and MS of fractions 2–5, 7, 8, 11, 14; fractions 1, 6; and fractions 10, 12, 13, 15 are very similar, inferring that the differences in their retention on C18 column are probably attributed to differences in their surface-attached functionalities. In summary, we postulate that the absorption of the C-dots species are MS dependent which is similar to that of gold NP [42] and other C-dots [32–35]; however, other factors including surface functionality and heteroatoms also play important roles [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL analysis of HPLC C-dots fractions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S4A displays the representative HPLC chromatogram of the C-dots sample synthesized with 1.0g chitosan, 15mL 2.0% acetic acid and 15-min microwave-heating. For comparison, a water sample was injected (curve a) and no peak is observed. By contrast, numerous well separated peaks are found in the absorption chromatogram (curve b) of the C-dots sample. No peaks are eluted after 29min. Although UHPLC can greatly shorten separation time and improve separation efficiency, the separation components are insufficient for subsequent PL and MS analyses. Thus, HPLC was applied to collect the separated C-dots for further characterization. All the peaks labeled as 1–15 in Fig. S4A are collected for PL and MALDI-TOF MS analyses (vide infra). The quantities of the other fractions are too small to be characterized by PL and MS and are not studied here.

Fig. S4B displays the UV–visible absorption spectra of HPLC fractions 1–15 (from bottom to top), revealing the spectral characteristics of these C-dots fractions and are consistent that of the UHPLC fractions (Fig. 4B). Similarly, fractions 2–5, 7, 8, 11, 14; fractions 10, 12, 13, 15; fractions 1, 6; and fraction 9 possess similar absorption spectral features which are consistent with that of the UHPLC fractions and the MS analysis results. That is, HPLC can serve as a supplementary tool for collecting fractions for further research.

Fig. 5 displays the PL spectra of fractions 1–15 at different λ
ex . No obvious shift of the PL bands is observed which is contrary to the λ
ex -dependent PL spectral behavior of the C-dots sample mixture. The PL bands are red-shifted from 427 to 445nm for fractions 1–8 and at 440nm for fraction 9. No obvious shift of the PL bands is observed for fractions 10–14 and 15, having the PL bands centered at 436nm. In addition, the PL band of fraction 14 is at 431nm, while the PL bands of the C-dots sample are bathochromatically shifted from 431 to 482nm with the λ
ex switching from 280 to 400nm (Fig. S1B). These results indicate that the original C-dots sample is indeed multicomponent in nature while the separated fractions appear to be in higher purity. In summary, HPLC is a useful supplementary tool to collect C-dots fractions while UHPLC is capable of separating complex mixtures of C-dots and the separated C-dots species can be truly and accurately registered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI-TOF MS analysis of HPLC C-dots fractions
ce:section.ce:para: Mass spectrometry has been an indispensable tool in biology and chemistry to determine the mass of C-dots. However, MALDI-TOF MS has its limitation as it may not accurately determine the highest mass ions of C-dots. Nevertheless, it was applied to characterize HPLC fractions 1–15 as depicted in Fig. 6. The “possible” highest mass C-dots species are labeled on all the MS with red asterisks. All the peak assignments are within the mass accuracy of the MALDI-TOF MS, i.e., 1–2 mass units. The highest mass ions in the MS approximately indicate the relative molecular mass of the C-dots species, ranging from 2756 to 4131Da for fractions 1–15. The RP-HPLC elution order basically follows the molecular mass of separated C-dots species with some exceptions. It is well known that C-dots are composed of sp 2-hybridized carbon cores [43,44]. The increase in core size also indicates the increase in the number of carbons of the C-dots. In addition, it has been reported that particle size is proportional to the cube root of the mass of NP [45]. As such, stronger interaction between C-dots and C18 stationary phase takes place when the molecular mass of C-dots increases under RP-HPLC condition. It is plausible that the separation is not only governed by molecular mass but also the shape and surface-functionality of the C-dots.

Although MALDI-TOF MS cannot accurately assign the highest mass ions of C-dots fractions, it is still a valuable tool to elucidate the surface-attached functionalities of NP by examining their fragmentation patterns [42,46]. As indicated by Fig. 6, it is interesting to observe regular mass spacing in the MS of the fractions derived from the chitosan unit or its dehydration products. It is possible that the C-dots species are mainly comprised carbon cores and some surface-attached chitosan moieties. The insets of Fig. 6 display the expanded MS to study the major mass spacing of the fractions. Fractions 1 and 6 contain a series of major m/z spacing in the alternating mass spacing of 175 and 163. The 175 mass spacing corresponds to the –NH2 in one opening pyranose ring chitosan which reacts with a CH3COOH molecule with the loss of –CH2OH and liberates as N-(3,4-dihydroxy-1-oxopentan-yl)acetamide. The 163 mass spacing corresponds to one opening pyranose ring chitosan released as 2-amino-3,4,6-trihydroxyhexanal. Similarly, fractions 2–5, 7, 8, 11, and 14 display the major m/z spacing in the alternating mass units of 115 and 169. The 115 mass spacing corresponds to one opening pyranose ring chitosan dehydrated with the loss of H2O and –CH2OH to produce 2-amino-3-hydroxypent-4-enal. The 169 mass spacing corresponds to one opening pyranose ring chitosan dehydrated with the loss of two H2O molecules and its –NH2 group reacts with a CH3COOH molecule to release N-(6-hydroxy-1-oxohexa-3,4-dien-2-yl)acetamide. Fraction 9 shows a series of major m/z spacing in the alternating mass spacing of 187 and 169. The 187 mass spacing corresponds to one opening pyranose ring chitosan dehydrated with the loss of one H2O molecule and its –NH2 group reacts with a CH3COOH molecule to release (E)-N-(3,6-dihydroxy-1-oxohex-4-en-2-yl)acetamide.

Finally, fractions 10, 12, 13, and 15 possess the major m/z spacing of 119 and 177 that are different from fractions 1–9, 11, and 14. The 119 mass spacing corresponds to one opening pyranose ring chitosan lost –CH2CH2OH to produce 2-amino-3,4-dihydroxybutanal. The 177 mass spacing corresponds to the –NH2 in one opening pyranose ring chitosan which reacts with a CH3COOH molecule with the loss of –CHO and liberates as N-(2,3,5-trihydroxypentyl)acetamide. The proposed chemical structures of the fragments are displayed in the inset of Fig. 6.

The MS data further confirm our IR spectrum that the pyranose ring chitosan reacts readily with acetic acid with a concomitant degradation of the chitosan chain and the pyranose ring. Under microwave-assisted heating, the pyranose ring chitosan reacts with acetic acid through the hydrothermal carbonization process. In addition, some minor m/z spacings are observed in each group of fragments as indicated in Fig. S5. The mass differences of these minor peaks are 12, 15, 16, 17, 18 or 44 corresponding to the mass loss of a carbon (C) atom, a methyl (CH3), an amine (NH2), a hydroxyl (OH) group, a water (H2O) molecule, or an amide (O=C–NH2) group, respectively. Those are commonly found in each group of fragment ions of fractions 1–15. The loss of hydroxymethyl (–CH2OH) functionality is also common to the fractions attributing to its surface-attachment to the analogous sphere of carbon core [8,20,47,48]. In summary, our MS concur with the IR data (vide supra) that the C-dots species should comprise carbon cores with surface-attached NH2, OH, and CH2OH functionalities.

In this study, C-dots have been synthesized from a fast, simple and “green” route with chitosan and glacial acetic acid as precursors. A UHPLC methodology has been developed and applied successfully to evaluate the as-synthesized C-dots samples from various experimental conditions. The main contribution of this work is to demonstrate that UHPLC is able to separate complex mixtures of C-dots samples within 16min. The significances of UHPLC separation are shorter retention time and better resolution of C-dots. The complexity of a C-dot sample can be evaluated by the obtained chromatogram which is commonly ignored since only the PL properties of the C-dot samples are assessed.

The effect of chitosan, glacial acetic acid, and reaction time on the synthesis of C-dots was studied by UV absorption, PL spectroscopy, Φ
s coupled with modern analytical UHPLC methodology. It is found that appropriate chitosan and glacial acetic acid favor the production of C-dots with stronger emissions. The highest Φ
s of C-dots were studied by FTIR, XPS, TEM, UV absorption and PL spectroscopy. To better understand the fundamental properties of each C-dots species, HPLC fractions are collected and characterized by UV–visible absorption, PL spectroscopy, and MALDI-TOF MS. The chemical structure of C-dots is a carbon core covered with –NH2, –OH and –CH2OH functionalities. In essence, our developed UHPLC and HPLC methodology together with absorption and PL spectroscopy, and MS provides an effective way to determine the spectral feature, characteristics and structural features of C-dots fractions present in a sample. Unfortunately, the structural differences of C-dots are not identified in the present research. It is anticipated that more experimental work are required to illustrate their structural differences.

Our proposed UHPLC and RP-HPLC methods provide well-defined peaks and should open new, convenient and rapid avenues to isolate different C-dots fractions for their characterization, catalysis, photophysical and other studies. Currently, the as-synthesized C-dots products are far from ideal in terms of purity, leaving plenty of room for the development of better analytical separation techniques to more precisely harvest various C-dots fractions for specific applications.

We would express our sincere thanks to Ms Winnie Y.K. Wu of the Institute of Advanced Materials for taking the TEM images and XPS, and Ms Silva T. Mo of the Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University for acquiring the MALDI-TOF MS. The TEM was supported by the Special Equipment Grant from the University Grants Committee of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Grant SEG_HKBU06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.08.051.
